items.front: How should ATC handle aircraft engaged in flight inspection of NAVAIDs?
items.back: Provide maximum assistance, maintain direct contact unless otherwise agreed, and exchange information about known traffic and the pilot's intentions.

items.front: What assistance do flight inspection aircraft receive?
items.back: MAXIMUM assistance. Maintain direct contact with the pilot and exchange information on known traffic and intentions. Per 9-1-1.

items.front: What does 'recorded run' mean for a Flight Check aircraft?
items.back: It means the automated inspection is in progress and the run should not be interrupted unless necessary, because interruption usually requires the run to be started over.

items.front: What is the basic ATC priority for Flight Check aircraft?
items.back: Provide special handling and expedite the inspection mission, recognizing that preplanned automatic flight limits the pilot's ability to comply with normal ATC changes.

items.front: What restriction applies when giving route or altitude changes to aircraft carrying dangerous materials?
items.back: Advise the pilot of the proposed change and why it is needed; if giving an en route descent, do not vector the aircraft off its planned route unless the pilot concurs.

items.front: What is required for operations in the DC SFRA security services area?
items.back: Two-way radio communications, flight planning, and an operational transponder on an assigned code are required.

items.front: What must a controller do upon receiving a SECNOT?
items.back: Forward all information on the aircraft to the OS or CIC. If information is not known, broadcast the call sign on all frequencies and advise the OS or CIC of any response.

items.front: How are law enforcement aircraft handled when they request assistance?
items.back: Provide maximum assistance and, when requested by the crew, use a separate and unique communications frequency whenever possible.

items.front: What block-altitude handling applies when authorizing military aerial refueling?
items.back: Authorize refueling along the published or special track and assign the requested refueling block, either as a maintained block or as a block beginning at one altitude and descending to another.

items.front: What is the preferred way to separate two supersonic aircraft above FL600?
items.back: Normally descend the aircraft at the lower flight level and provide vertical separation.

items.front: What advance notice for supersonic flight above FL600?
items.back: 16 hours in advance of proposed departure time for processing and approval by ARTCCs. Per 9-2-14(a).

items.front: When are military special-use frequencies assigned?
items.back: Assign them to the listed qualifying military operations, including all aircraft during supersonic flight.

items.front: What must ATC do if a proposed flight path will cross a reported or forecast hazardous-radiation area?
items.back: Advise the pilot and reroute the aircraft if the pilot requests it.

items.front: How are SAMP flights handled?
items.back: Provide special handling and approve inflight route and altitude changes to the maximum extent possible, even if other IFR aircraft must be recleared.

items.front: What is the ATC priority for AWACS or NORAD Special flights?
items.back: Do not delay them; expediting actions may include minor route changes and altitude changes within plus or minus 2,000 feet that do not impede progress to the orbit.

items.front: What traffic information is given to a TEAL or NOAA mission aircraft for dropsonde releases when workload permits?
items.back: Advise of traffic expected to pass below the mission aircraft near the drop, including the traffic's altitude, direction of flight, and ETA at the closest point or drop fix.

items.front: When may celestial navigation training be approved in the conterminous United States?
items.back: Only for USAF or USN aircraft that are transponder-equipped and operating within ARTCC radar coverage.

items.front: When may an evasive action maneuver be approved?
items.back: Only when traffic conditions permit it, and ATC specifies the route segment, maximum centerline deviation, and altitude limits as needed.

items.front: What must controllers know before handling nonstandard formation or cell operations?
items.back: They should know the tactics used in the nonstandard cell operation and the procedures that support it.

items.front: Once special handling is approved for an experimental aircraft, when may ATC ask it to deviate from planned action?
items.back: Only to preclude an emergency situation.

items.front: When may special procedures be approved for FAA research and development test flights?
items.back: Only when they are coordinated in advance, traffic permits, and the facility air traffic manager has approved the special procedures.

items.front: What must the first facility contacting a FLYNET aircraft do?
items.back: Provide expeditious handling, report it to the OS or CIC for DEN reporting, and note in the flight plan remarks that DEN notification has been completed.

items.front: When does MARSA separation responsibility shift on designated IFR military training routes?
items.back: For designated MARSA routes, the military assumes separation responsibility after the aircraft passes the entry fix until ATC reestablishes separation after the MTR operation is complete.

items.front: How should interceptor aircraft on active air defense scrambles be handled?
items.back: Provide maximum assistance and, when requested, expedite transfer of control jurisdiction to the requesting ADCF.

items.front: What must ATC do with reports of unusual aircraft activity near special interest sites?
items.back: Immediately relay the information to supervisory or CIC personnel.

items.front: What is the key rule about Special Flight Rules Areas?
items.back: Special flight rules are prescribed in 14 CFR part 93, and all SFRAs are subject to those special rules unless ATC authorizes otherwise.

items.front: When do the Chapter 9 special-use airspace procedures not apply?
items.back: They do not apply when the pilot already has using-agency permission, the using agency gives permission, the airspace has been released to the controlling agency, or the aircraft is on an approved ALTRV as specified.

items.front: How are nonparticipating aircraft separated from active SUA, ATCAAs, or stationary ALTRVs?
items.back: Assign an altitude at least 500 feet above or below the active airspace, or 1,000 feet above FL290, unless an LOA or LOP provides another method.

items.front: What VFR-on-top instruction is used if a route may enter active SUA or an ATCAA?
items.back: Tell the pilot to maintain VFR-on-top at least 500 feet above the upper limit or below the lower limit of the airspace, or clear the aircraft by another route that provides approved separation.

items.front: What if a using agency grants permission to transit active SUA or an ATCAA under an LOA or LOP?
items.back: Comply with the using agency's instructions or clearances and provide the required separation minima between aircraft in transit.

items.front: What information must ATC determine when an aircraft plans to dump fuel?
items.back: Determine the route and altitude to be flown and the weather conditions in which the dumping will occur.

items.front: When may ATC request a different route for fuel dumping?
items.back: An aircraft dumping fuel may be asked to fly a different route unless the dumping is for emergency reasons.

items.front: What altitude must be assigned when an aircraft dumps fuel in IFR conditions?
items.back: At least 2,000 feet above the highest obstacle within 5 miles of the route or pattern being flown.

items.front: What altitude for fuel dumping in IFR conditions?
items.back: At least 2,000 feet above the highest obstacle within 5 miles of the route or pattern being flown. Per 9-4-3.

items.front: How are VFR radar-identified aircraft separated from an aircraft dumping fuel?
items.back: Separate them by 5 miles and in accordance with the applicable radar procedures.

items.front: What separation from a fuel-dumping aircraft?
items.back: IFR: 1,000 feet above (or Chapter 4 minima if greater), 2,000 feet below, or 5 miles radar. Per 9-4-4(a)(1-3).

items.front: How long are fuel-dumping advisories broadcast and how often?
items.back: Facilities concerned broadcast a fuel-dumping advisory every 3 minutes until the operation stops, then broadcast a termination advisory.

items.front: What broadcast interval during fuel dumping?
items.back: Every 3 minutes until the dumping stops. Inform other controllers and facilities that might be concerned. Per 9-4-5(a).

items.front: When may ATC vector aircraft for jettisoning external stores?
items.back: Only at locations where a drop area is established and, for civil operators or companies, only when a written agreement is in effect.

items.front: What advance notice must an unmanned free-balloon operator give ATC before descent?
items.back: The operator must advise ATC 1 hour before descent.

items.front: How often is a derelict-balloon advisory broadcast after radar contact is lost?
items.back: Broadcast the advisory every 10 minutes until the derelict balloon is no longer a factor.

items.front: When is coordination required for parachute jumps?
items.back: Coordinate pertinent information before and at the end of each jump or series of jumps that begins or ends in your area of jurisdiction.

items.front: How are parachute operations handled in Class A, B, and C airspace?
items.back: Authorize them only within the designated jump airspace, separate nonparticipating aircraft from that airspace, and impose any needed conditions or restrictions.

items.front: How are parachute jump requests handled in Class D airspace with a functioning tower?
items.back: Authorize the jump with respect to known or observed traffic and issue advisory information as needed for safe conduct of the operation.

items.front: What advisories are given to known nonparticipating aircraft for parachute operations in other control airspace?
items.back: Issue the jump location, time, duration, and the altitude from which the jump will be made.

items.front: What must be done with reported or observed UAP or unexplained phenomena activity?
items.back: Inform the operations supervisor or CIC.
